summary: Gruber dissects a Wall Street Journal article by Ben Charny about the iPhone's lack of Flash support, finding it riddled with factual errors, poor sourcing, and a fundamentally flawed premise. He notes the article was quietly edited after publication to remove embarrassing claims, including calling Apple's stock 'beleaguered' when it was up 45% year-over-year. Gruber argues the article completely misses the core power dynamic: Adobe needs the iPhone far more than Apple needs Flash, because no competing device can match the iPhone's mobile web browsing. He concludes that Apple has strategic reason to resist Flash, as it has the opportunity to establish QuickTime and H.264 as the de facto standards for mobile video.
key_points: The WSJ article contains multiple factual errors, including the claim that Adobe 'dropped support' for the Mac and that YouTube is the only source of H.264 video on the web
The article was stealth-edited after publication to remove the word 'beleaguered' describing Apple's stock and a claim that iPhone sales were hurt by the lack of Flash
The article's central flaw is misunderstanding the power dynamic: Adobe needs Flash on the iPhone, but no competing Flash-capable device threatens iPhone sales
Apple has a strategic incentive to block Flash and establish H.264/QuickTime as the mobile video standard
The article relies on unnamed forum posts as sourcing for a Wall Street Journal news report
money_quotes: Adobe is under pressure to get Flash on the iPhone because the iPhone is clearly the leading mobile device for web surfing, but Apple is under very little pressure to put Flash on the iPhone because there is no competing device that fits in this sentence: 'There are many people who were going to buy an iPhone, but, because it doesn't support Flash, bought a ———— instead.'
So YouTube is the only source for QuickTime or H.264 video on the entire web?
Adobe's patience appears to be wearing thin. And what happens then?
Apparently, unnamed web site forum posts are valid sources for Wall Street Journal news reports.
It's a bit rich to describe Apple's stock price as 'beleaguered' when it's still up 45 percent from a year ago.
They have the opportunity to establish QuickTime and H.264 as the de facto standards for mobile video on the web.
key_insight: The WSJ's Flash-on-iPhone story fundamentally misreads the power dynamic — Adobe needs the iPhone far more than Apple needs Flash, giving Apple both the leverage and the strategic incentive to push open video standards instead.
